questions.question: A factory produces 500 microchips or 100 sensors per hour. A technician optimizes the sensor line, increasing sensor output to 125 per hour while microchip output stays the same. How does the opportunity cost of one microchip change?
options: It decreases from 0.25 sensors to 0.2 sensors per microchip produced
It increases from 0.2 sensors to 0.25 sensors per microchip produced
It remains unchanged at 0.2 sensors because microchip output did not change
It becomes negative because the sensor line improvement increases total output
questions.explanation: Originally: 1 chip costs $100/500 = 0.2$ sensors. After optimization: 1 chip costs $125/500 = 0.25$ sensors. The opportunity cost of a microchip has increased.

questions.question: Country Alpha produces Steel in 10 hours and Aluminum in 20 hours. Country Beta produces Steel in 15 hours and Aluminum in 25 hours. Which country has the absolute advantage in Aluminum?
options: Country Beta, because its Steel and Aluminum production times are closer together
Country Alpha, because it requires fewer labor hours per ton of Aluminum than Beta
Country Beta, because its opportunity cost of Aluminum is lower than Alpha's
Neither country, because each has an advantage in a different metal product
questions.explanation: Country Alpha needs 20 hours per ton of Aluminum vs. Country Beta's 25 hours. Fewer hours per unit means higher absolute productivity in Aluminum.

questions.question: Using the data (Alpha: 10h/Steel, 20h/Aluminum; Beta: 15h/Steel, 25h/Aluminum), which country has the comparative advantage in Steel?
options: Country Beta, because it uses more total labor hours for Steel production
Country Alpha, because its opportunity cost of Steel is 0.5 Aluminum versus Beta's 0.6
Country Beta, because its Steel and Aluminum industries are more balanced
Country Alpha, because it produces both Steel and Aluminum more efficiently
questions.explanation: Alpha's cost of 1 Steel = $10/20 = 0.5$ Aluminum. Beta's cost = $15/25 = 0.6$ Aluminum. Alpha has the lower relative cost in Steel.

questions.question: An earthquake destroys 30% of a nation's capital stock across all sectors of the economy. How is the Production Possibility Frontier affected?
options: The PPF rotates outward along the axis of the most capital-intensive good
The PPF shifts inward toward the origin, reducing feasible output of all goods
The PPF remains unchanged because labor, not capital, determines production
The PPF shifts outward as the country rebuilds with newer, more efficient capital
questions.explanation: A reduction in a general factor of production decreases the maximum possible output of all goods, shifting the entire PPF inward toward the origin.

questions.question: Unit labor requirements are $a_{LX} = 8$ and $a_{LY} = 2$. What is the autarky relative price $P_Y/P_X$?
options: 4, calculated as the ratio of the larger labor requirement to the smaller one
0.25, equal to the ratio of the unit labor requirements $a_{LY}/a_{LX}$
8, matching the absolute labor hours required to produce Good X
16, the product of both unit labor requirements multiplied together
questions.explanation: Autarky relative price $P_Y/P_X = a_{LY}/a_{LX} = 2/8 = 0.25$. One unit of Y costs 0.25 units of X.

questions.question: What does a flat horizontal section of the World Relative Supply curve represent in the Ricardian model?
options: The world price at which both countries have fully specialized in one good
The opportunity cost of one trading country, where it is indifferent between the two goods
The maximum possible world output of the good measured on the horizontal axis
The equilibrium world price determined by the intersection with relative demand
questions.explanation: A flat step occurs when the world price equals a country's domestic opportunity cost. At that price, the country gains nothing from specializing and can produce any mix.

questions.question: World Relative Demand intersects World Relative Supply on a vertical segment. What does this imply about the two trading countries?
options: One country has completely exited the global market for that traded good
Both countries are fully specialized in their respective comparative advantage goods
Neither country finds trade to be beneficial at the prevailing world equilibrium price
Production is divided equally between the two goods in each of the two nations
questions.explanation: Vertical RS segments occur when relative quantity is fixed because all labor in both countries is dedicated to producing their specialized export goods.

questions.question: A Home worker is 4 times as productive as a Foreign worker in Industry A, but the Home wage is 5 times the Foreign wage. Who has the cost advantage in Industry A?
options: Home, because its absolute productivity is four times higher than Foreign's
Foreign, because Home's wage premium exceeds its productivity advantage
Both have equal unit labor costs because the ratios are nearly identical
Neither has an advantage; trade in Industry A will not occur between them
questions.explanation: Home's productivity advantage ($4\times$) is smaller than its wage premium ($5\times$), giving Home higher unit labor costs. Foreign has the cost advantage.

questions.question: A country opens to trade and specializes in its comparative advantage good. What happens to the real wage measured in terms of the imported good?
options: The real wage falls because imports become more expensive after trade opens
The real wage rises because the relative price of the imported good decreases
The real wage remains constant because it is determined solely by domestic MPL
The real wage fluctuates unpredictably depending on exchange rate movements
questions.explanation: The nominal wage is tied to export productivity and price. Since imports become relatively cheaper, purchasing power over imported goods rises.

questions.question: The Ricardian model shows that a country can benefit from trade even when it is less productive in every single industry. Why is this possible?
options: Less productive countries always receive technology transfers from their trading partners
Trade depends on relative productivity differences, not on absolute productivity levels
International law guarantees that every country gains equally from free trade agreements
Countries with lower productivity inevitably have lower wages, giving them an advantage everywhere
questions.explanation: As long as internal opportunity costs differ between countries, there is a basis for mutually beneficial exchange based on comparative, not absolute, advantage.

questions.question: Why does trade allow a country to consume a bundle of goods that lies outside its Production Possibility Frontier?
options: Trade increases the country's total labor supply through international immigration
The world relative price differs from the domestic opportunity cost, enabling a better exchange rate
Governments subsidize consumption when trade is opened to ensure everyone benefits
Trade always shifts the PPF outward, allowing more of both goods to be produced
questions.explanation: Trading at the world price allows the country to exchange its export good for imports at a more favorable rate than domestic production would permit.

questions.question: A trade policy makes one group \$100 richer and another group \$10 poorer, with no compensation paid to the losers. How is this outcome classified?
options: An actual Pareto Improvement because the total gains exceed the total losses
A Potential Pareto Improvement, since the winners could compensate the losers and remain ahead
Neither an improvement nor a deterioration because the net effect is ambiguous
A Kaldor-Hicks deterioration because at least one group was made strictly worse off
questions.explanation: Winners ($+\$100$) could compensate losers ($-\$10$) and still gain $\$90$. This makes it a Potential Pareto Improvement (Kaldor-Hicks efficient), but not an actual one without compensation.

questions.question: At a Pareto Efficient allocation in an exchange economy, what must be true about all consumers?
options: Each consumer must have exactly the same total income and wealth level
All consumers must have identical Marginal Rates of Substitution between goods
Every consumer must consume exactly the same physical bundle of goods
The government must have redistributed income to achieve perfect equality
questions.explanation: If MRS values differed between any two consumers, there would still exist mutually beneficial trades between them. Equalized MRS means all such trades are exhausted.

questions.question: $P_X = 20$, $a_{LX} = 4$, $P_Y = 30$, and $a_{LY} = 5$ with perfectly mobile labor. Where will workers choose to be employed?
options: In Sector X, because the price per unit of Good X is lower and more affordable
In Sector Y, where the hourly wage of 6 exceeds Sector X's wage of only 5
In Sector X, because its lower unit labor requirement signals higher productivity
Workers will split evenly because the wage gap between sectors is too small
questions.explanation: Wage in X = $20/4 = 5$. Wage in Y = $30/5 = 6$. Rational workers migrate to the higher-paying Sector Y until Sector X production ceases.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, the relative price equals the opportunity cost: $P_X/P_Y = a_{LX}/a_{LY}$. What is the state of production in this economy?
options: The country must fully specialize in producing only Good X for export
The country can produce any combination of X and Y along its entire PPF
Production of both goods must cease because neither is profitable at that price
The country must split its labor force exactly equally between the two goods
questions.explanation: When the price ratio equals the opportunity cost, wages are equal in both sectors. Labor is indifferent, and the production point can be anywhere on the PPF.

questions.question: A Ricardian country has 600 workers and the unit labor requirement for Good X is $a_{LX} = 2$. What is the maximum possible output of Good X?
options: 1,200 units, calculated by multiplying total labor by the unit labor requirement
300 units, equal to the total labor endowment divided by the unit labor requirement
600 units, matching the total number of available workers in the country
2 units, reflecting the inverse of the unit labor requirement times the labor force
questions.explanation: Maximum X = $L / a_{LX} = 600 \div 2 = 300$ units. This is the X-axis intercept of the PPF.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, the price of an export good rises by 20%. What happens to the nominal return of factors specific to that export sector?
options: Their nominal return rises by less than 20% because some gains go to consumers
Their nominal return rises by exactly 20%, moving in proportion to the output price
Their nominal return remains unchanged because factor returns are fixed by contracts
Their nominal return rises by more than 20% due to the Magnification Effect
questions.explanation: Because factors cannot relocate to other sectors, their nominal return is tied directly and proportionally to the price of the specific good they produce.

questions.question: Why does the immobile factor model predict sharp political conflict over trade policy?
options: Because trade benefits are so large that everyone fights over their share of the gains
Income changes are sector-specific and cannot be equalized through factor reallocation
The model predicts that trade reduces total national income, creating universal opposition
Immobile factor models assume that all factors benefit equally, eliminating any conflict
questions.explanation: Export sector factors gain while import-competing factors lose. Without factor mobility, there is no mechanism to spread or equalize these gains and losses across sectors.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, all goods prices in the economy rise uniformly by 10%. What is the effect on the real income of all factor owners?
options: Real income falls because higher prices reduce purchasing power for everyone
Real income remains unchanged because nominal returns also rise by exactly 10%
Real income rises for capital owners but falls for workers in all sectors
Real income falls for import-competing factors but rises for export sector factors
questions.explanation: All nominal returns rise by 10%, exactly matching the increase in the cost of living. Real purchasing power remains unchanged for every factor owner in every sector.

questions.question: Country A has a Capital-to-Labor ratio of 0.8. Country B has a ratio of 0.5. Which country is relatively labor-abundant?
options: Country A, because it has a larger absolute stock of capital than Country B
Country B, because a lower K/L ratio means it has more labor per unit of capital
Country A, because its higher K/L ratio makes its labor more productive overall
Neither country, because both have K/L ratios below 1.0 by definition
questions.explanation: Country B's K/L ratio ($0.5$) is lower than Country A's ($0.8$). This means Country B has more labor relative to its capital stock and is labor-abundant.

questions.question: According to the Stolper-Samuelson theorem, a rise in the price of the capital-intensive good will have what effect on factor returns?
options: Both real wages and real capital rents will increase by equal percentages
The real rental rate of capital will rise while the real wage of labor will fall
Only labor's real wage will rise while capital's return remains unchanged
The theorem makes no clear prediction about factor returns or income distribution
questions.explanation: The factor used intensively in the price-rising sector (capital) gains in real terms. The other factor (labor) loses in real terms according to the theorem.

questions.question: What was the central empirical finding of the Leontief Paradox?
options: Trade between similar countries exceeded trade between countries with different endowments
The capital-abundant United States appeared to export labor-intensive goods
Factor prices had fully equalized between the US and its major trading partners
Comparative advantage could not explain trade in agricultural products at all
questions.explanation: Leontief's 1953 study found US exports were less capital-intensive than US imports, contradicting the H-O prediction for a capital-rich country.

questions.question: In the Heckscher-Ohlin model, why does international trade lead toward factor price equalization?
options: Workers migrate en masse from low-wage to high-wage countries seeking better pay
Trade in goods serves as an indirect substitute for the international movement of factors
Central banks coordinate monetary policy to equalize wages and interest rates worldwide
The World Trade Organization mandates uniform wages across all of its member countries
questions.explanation: By exporting goods intensive in the abundant factor, a country indirectly exports that factor's services. This raises domestic demand for the abundant factor, pulling its price toward the global level.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, an increase in the total supply of mobile labor will have what effect?
options: Nominal wages rise while output in both sectors decreases due to overcrowding
Nominal wages fall while output in both sectors expands at the now lower labor cost
Nominal wages remain constant while only the agricultural sector expands its output
Nominal wages rise in manufacturing but fall in agriculture as labor is reallocated
questions.explanation: More labor shifts the supply curve outward, lowering the equilibrium wage. At the lower wage, both sectors find it profitable to hire more workers and expand output.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, $P_M$ rises while $P_A$ remains constant. Which group unambiguously gains?
options: Mobile labor, because nominal wages always rise faster than goods prices
Owners of capital specific to Manufacturing, due to the Magnification Effect
Landowners in Agriculture, because higher manufacturing prices boost the whole economy
All factors gain equally because trade benefits every factor of production
questions.explanation: Capital's rental rate rises more than $P_M$ (Magnification Effect). The nominal wage rises by less than $P_M$. Capital owners in Manufacturing unambiguously gain in real terms.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, $P_M$ rises while $P_A$ remains constant. What happens to the real wage measured in terms of the Agricultural good?
options: The real wage falls because manufactured goods now cost more to consume
The real wage rises because $W$ increases while $P_A$ stays unchanged
The real wage remains constant because wages are anchored to agricultural prices
The real wage is unaffected because workers only consume manufactured goods
questions.explanation: The nominal wage $W$ rises due to higher labor demand in Manufacturing. Since $P_A$ is unchanged, $W/P_A$ increases, meaning workers can buy more agricultural goods.

questions.question: How is the equilibrium wage determined in the Specific Factor Model?
options: By government minimum wage legislation applied uniformly across all sectors
At the intersection where $P_M \times MPL_M = P_A \times MPL_A$, equalizing VMPL across sectors
By the average productivity of labor across all industries in the entire economy
At the point where the wage equals the price of the most expensive good produced
questions.explanation: Labor is allocated between sectors until the Value of Marginal Product is equalized. At this point, there is no incentive for further labor reallocation.

questions.question: A firm's average costs fall as its own output increases, but costs do not depend on the overall size of the industry. What type of economies does this firm have?
options: External economies of scale, because the firm benefits from its own expansion
Internal economies of scale, because costs depend on the firm's own production volume
Constant returns to scale, because costs fall in proportion to output growth
Decreasing returns to scale, because larger firms always have coordination problems
questions.explanation: Internal economies of scale depend on the size of the individual firm, not the industry. They often lead to imperfect competition as larger firms have cost advantages.

questions.question: In a monopolistic competition trade model, what is the primary source of consumer welfare gain from trade?
options: Lower prices caused by more intense competition from foreign rival firms
Access to a greater variety of differentiated products produced in different countries
Higher wages as export sector expansion increases the demand for domestic labor
Reduced income inequality as trade benefits are spread evenly across all workers
questions.explanation: Trade allows consumers to choose from both domestic and foreign varieties of differentiated products. Even without price changes, access to more varieties increases consumer utility.

questions.question: Why do industries with external economies of scale tend to concentrate geographically in specific regions?
options: Government tax incentives require firms to locate in designated industrial zones
Firms benefit from specialized local labor pools, suppliers, and knowledge spillovers
Natural resources are distributed unevenly, forcing all firms to locate near them
Transportation costs are zero within regions, making any location equally efficient
questions.explanation: External economies arise from the size of the entire industry in a location. Geographic clustering creates thick labor markets, specialized suppliers, and informal knowledge exchange.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a tariff and an import quota for a small country?
options: Tariffs always restrict more imports than quotas at the same nominal rate of protection
The distribution of economic rents: tariffs generate revenue, quotas create license rents
Quotas are always more efficient because they provide certainty about import volumes
There is no meaningful difference because both policies have identical economic effects
questions.explanation: A tariff generates government revenue automatically. A quota creates quota rents that go to license holders unless the government auctions the import licenses.

questions.question: Under what condition can a large country's import tariff potentially improve its national welfare?
options: The tariff must be set high enough to completely eliminate all imports of the good
The terms-of-trade gain from lower world prices must exceed the domestic deadweight loss
Any positive tariff always improves welfare for a large country regardless of the rate
The tariff must be accompanied by equivalent export subsidies to balance the effects
questions.explanation: A large country can force foreign exporters to accept lower pre-tariff prices. If that terms-of-trade gain exceeds the efficiency loss, national welfare rises.

questions.question: Why is a Voluntary Export Restraint typically more costly to the importing country than an equivalent tariff?
options: VERs always restrict more imports than tariffs at the same nominal protection level
The quota rents are captured by foreign exporting firms rather than the domestic government
VERs are administered by international organizations that charge high fees to importers
Tariffs generate more government revenue that can be redistributed to domestic consumers
questions.explanation: Because the exporting country administers the VER, foreign firms restrict supply and raise prices, capturing the resulting scarcity rent. This worsens the importing country's terms of trade.

questions.question: What does the Theory of the Second Best suggest about using tariffs when domestic market failures exist?
options: Free trade remains the optimal policy regardless of any domestic market distortions
A tariff may partially improve welfare if it counteracts a pre-existing domestic distortion
Tariffs are always the most efficient way to correct any type of market failure
The theory proves that protectionism is always superior to free trade in every case
questions.explanation: When one market distortion already exists, adding another (like a tariff) may partially offset it. The tariff is a second-best tool, inferior to directly addressing the original distortion.

questions.question: A \$5 specific tariff is applied to a good with a world price of \$50. What is the equivalent ad valorem tariff rate?
options: 5%, calculated as the tariff divided by the domestic price after the tax
10%, equal to the specific tariff amount divided by the world price of the good
\$5, because specific tariffs are always expressed in absolute dollar terms only
50%, reflecting the ratio of the world price to the specific tariff per unit
questions.explanation: Ad valorem equivalent = $(\text{Tariff} \div \text{World Price}) \times 100 = (\$5 \div \$50) \times 100 = 10\%$.

questions.question: A small country imposes a 15% import tariff. What happens to the domestic price of the imported good?
options: The price rises by less than 15% because some of the tariff is absorbed by foreigners
The price increases by exactly 15%, matching the full ad valorem tariff rate
The price remains at the world level because small countries cannot affect prices
The price rises by more than 15% due to additional markups by domestic distributors
questions.explanation: A small country is a price taker on world markets. It cannot influence the world price, so the full tariff is added to the world price and passed through to domestic consumers.

questions.question: On a supply-demand diagram, which area represents the consumption distortion deadweight loss caused by a tariff?
options: The rectangle representing the total government tariff revenue from remaining imports
The triangle under the demand curve between the free-trade and tariff-restricted quantities
The trapezoid capturing the transfer of surplus from consumers to domestic producers
The area between the world price line and the domestic price line above imports
questions.explanation: This triangle captures the welfare loss from consumers who are priced out of the market by the tariff. Their lost surplus is not captured by anyone else.

questions.question: When does the Effective Rate of Protection differ from the nominal tariff rate?
options: When the good is not produced domestically and there is no competing industry
When tariff rates on intermediate inputs differ from the tariff rate on the final good
When the country is large enough to influence the world price through its tariff
The effective rate and nominal rate are always identical by mathematical definition
questions.explanation: The Effective Rate of Protection accounts for tariffs on both the final product and its imported inputs. It measures the protection provided to the domestic value-added process.

questions.question: The government auctions import licenses under a quota system. What will the total auction revenue equal?
options: The world price multiplied by the total quantity of imports allowed under the quota
The per-unit quota rent (domestic price minus world price) multiplied by the quota quantity
The administrative costs of running the government auction and enforcement process
Zero, because competitive bidding between firms drives the license price to nothing
questions.explanation: Bidders will pay up to the expected profit per unit imported. Competitive bidding drives the license price to the full per-unit quota rent, which the government collects.

questions.question: What is an export subsidy in international trade policy?
options: A tax reduction for domestic firms that import raw materials used in production
A direct government payment to a firm or individual for each unit shipped abroad
A low-interest loan provided by the government to foreign buyers of domestic goods
A rebate of customs duties paid on imported components that are later re-exported
questions.explanation: An export subsidy is a direct financial incentive paid to domestic producers for each unit they sell in foreign markets, encouraging exports over domestic sales.

questions.question: What does a local content requirement mandate for producers selling goods in the domestic market?
options: All imported goods must include a label showing their country of origin clearly
A specified fraction of a final good's value must be sourced from domestic production
Foreign firms must establish a local subsidiary before being permitted to export
Imported goods must meet the same testing standards as domestically produced goods
questions.explanation: Local content requirements protect domestic input suppliers by requiring manufacturers to use a minimum percentage of locally sourced components in their finished products.

questions.question: What is the net national welfare effect of an export subsidy imposed by a small country?
options: Positive, because the subsidy helps domestic firms penetrate foreign markets
Negative, due to production and consumption distortions plus the fiscal cost to government
Zero, because gains to producers are exactly offset by losses to consumers and taxpayers
Uncertain, depending on whether the subsidy is specific or ad valorem in nature
questions.explanation: A small country cannot improve its terms of trade. The subsidy creates deadweight losses while costing the government revenue, producing an unambiguous net welfare loss.

questions.question: A country consistently runs a Current Account surplus. What does this indicate about its relationship with the rest of the world?
options: The country is a net borrower, accumulating debt owed to foreign creditors
The country is a net lender, using its excess earnings to acquire foreign assets
The country has a balanced Financial Account with no net international capital flows
The country is depleting its official foreign exchange reserve holdings over time
questions.explanation: A CA surplus means the country earns more from trade and investment than it spends. The excess is lent abroad or used to acquire foreign assets, making the country a net lender.

questions.question: What types of transactions are recorded in the Capital Account of the Balance of Payments?
options: Cross-border purchases and sales of stocks, bonds, and other financial instruments
Non-produced, non-financial assets like patents plus capital transfers such as debt forgiveness
Exports and imports of manufactured goods, raw materials, and commercial services
Interest payments, dividends, and employee compensation earned across national borders
questions.explanation: The Capital Account (narrowly defined) records non-financial assets (patents, copyrights, trademarks) and capital transfers (debt forgiveness, investment grants).

questions.question: What is the fundamental national income identity linking the Current Account to saving and investment?
options: $CA = I - S$, meaning the Current Account equals investment minus saving
$CA = S - I$, the portion of national saving not used for domestic investment
$CA = S + I$, the sum of all domestic saving and investment activity
$CA = I / S$, the ratio of domestic investment to national saving
questions.explanation: $CA = S - I$. A country's net foreign investment equals the portion of its national saving that is not absorbed by domestic investment spending.

questions.question: How does a depreciation of the domestic currency affect exports and imports?
options: Exports become more expensive abroad and imports become cheaper domestically
Exports become cheaper for foreign buyers while imports become more expensive at home
Both exports and imports become cheaper due to the lower value of the currency
A depreciation has no effect on trade because prices are set in long-term contracts
questions.explanation: A depreciation lowers the foreign-currency price of exports and raises the domestic-currency price of imports. This shift in relative prices helps improve the trade balance over time.

questions.question: The US Dollar interest rate is 4% and the Euro interest rate is 2%. According to Interest Rate Parity, what does the market expect about the Dollar?
options: The Dollar should appreciate by 2% against the Euro to equalize total returns
The Dollar is expected to depreciate by approximately 2% against the Euro
The exchange rate should remain unchanged because interest rates are independent
The Euro should depreciate by 2% to match the higher Dollar interest rate
questions.explanation: The higher-interest currency (Dollar) must be expected to depreciate by the interest differential ($4\% - 2\% = 2\%$) to equalize expected returns across currencies.

questions.question: What does the real exchange rate measure in international economics?
options: The official rate set by the central bank for all international monetary transactions
The relative price of baskets of goods in two countries, adjusted for the nominal exchange rate
The difference between the current spot exchange rate and the forward exchange rate
The exchange rate that would prevail if all countries used a single global currency
questions.explanation: The real exchange rate $q = (E \times P^*)/P$ adjusts the nominal rate for differences in national price levels, measuring relative purchasing power or competitiveness.

questions.question: According to Purchasing Power Parity theory, what should happen to a country's currency if it experiences higher inflation than its trading partners?
options: The currency should appreciate to offset the effects of higher domestic inflation
The currency should depreciate in proportion to the inflation rate differential
The currency should remain stable because inflation only affects domestic prices
The currency should appreciate in nominal terms but depreciate in real terms
questions.explanation: Higher domestic inflation erodes internal purchasing power. To maintain price equilibrium across borders, the currency must also lose external value through depreciation.

questions.question: What characterizes a managed float exchange rate system?
options: The exchange rate is permanently fixed by law and never allowed to change
Market forces primarily determine the rate, but the central bank intervenes occasionally to limit volatility
A committee of commercial bank CEOs meets each week to set the official exchange rate
Multiple countries share a single currency managed by a supranational central bank authority
questions.explanation: A managed float (dirty float) combines market-determined exchange rates with discretionary official intervention to smooth excessive short-term fluctuations.
